title: What's the Difference Between UI and UX Design?
excerpt: UI and UX are two of the most commonly confused terms in digital design. Understanding what they actually mean helps you have better conversations with designers, make smarter hiring decisions, and build better digital products. Here's the definitive explanation.
content:
If you've spent any time researching web or app design, you've inevitably come across the terms UI and UX. They're often grouped together ("UI/UX design") and sometimes used interchangeably — but they describe genuinely different things.

UX Design: The Experience

UX stands for User Experience. UX design is concerned with the overall experience a user has when interacting with a product. It's strategic, structural, and research-driven.

UX designers focus on:

  • Understanding user needs and behaviors (through research)

  • Defining user flows and information architecture

  • Wireframing and prototyping to test ideas before committing to visual design

  • Identifying and removing friction in the user journey

  • Ensuring the product actually helps users accomplish their goals


UX design is often invisible when done well. It's the reason you can find what you're looking for in an app without having to think about it.

UI Design: The Interface

UI stands for User Interface. UI design is concerned with the visual layer — the buttons, icons, typography, colors, and layout that a user actually sees and touches.

UI designers focus on:

  • Visual design and aesthetic quality

  • Design systems and component consistency

  • Color, typography, and iconography

  • Micro-interactions and animation

  • Making the interface beautiful and intuitive


UI design is what you see. UX design is what you feel.

How They Work Together

The best digital products need both. Great UX without polished UI feels functional but dull. Beautiful UI without solid UX looks impressive but frustrates users. The most successful digital products — think Spotify, Airbnb, Apple's apps — excel at both.

Do You Need a UX Designer, a UI Designer, or Both?

For most small-to-mid business projects, you'll work with a designer who does both (a UI/UX designer). For complex applications or enterprise platforms, separate specialists become valuable.
